topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a gavel strike. Dr. Albright was departing. My stomach clenched, a cold, tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I reread the subject line three times, the words blurring slightly. I scrolled back through the last few months of correspondence, every glowing compliment, every pointed critique, suddenly feeling like a heavy indictment. I watched my fingers hover over the keyboard, unable to type a coherent response, the silence of my room amplifying the frantic thumping in my ears. When I finally managed to click ‘delete’ on the draft, my hands were visibly shaking. The weight of my unfinished work, the looming void where guidance used to be, pressed down on my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 2]

I walked into the departmental common room, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead seeming to mock my stillness. Everyone was chatting, laughing, the easy camaraderie of colleagues a sharp contrast to the sudden, profound isolation settling over me. I clutched my worn notebook to my chest, the paper crinkling under my grip. When Professor Davies mentioned the news casually, his tone was light, but it felt like a spotlight had been unexpectedly switched on, illuminating every perceived failing in my academic trajectory. I managed a weak, tight smile that didn't reach my eyes. I wanted to shrink into the worn armchair, to become invisible, to somehow erase the evidence of my uncertainty. The air felt thick, suffocating, and I focused intently on the pattern of the carpet, trying desperately to anchor myself to something solid.

[story 3]

The coffee tasted bitter, acrid on my tongue. I stared out the window at the sprawling campus, the familiar architecture suddenly feeling alien and indifferent. The news had hit me like a physical blow—the end of my primary mentor. A wave of intense heat rushed up my neck, making my skin prickle. I picked up my laptop, the screen reflecting a pale, drawn expression. Every sentence I had written for the thesis felt suddenly hollow, inadequate, a flimsy structure ready to collapse under scrutiny. I started pacing the small space between my desk and the window, the movement jerky and uncontrolled. I kept catching my reflection in the dark glass; the pallor of my face, the way my shoulders hunched inward, spoke volumes louder than any spoken word could have.
